data.title: Groupon: This Marketplace Could Be At An Inflection Point (Upgrage)
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Earlier last year I wrote a bearish article on Groupon (GRPN). My main criticism of Groupon is that fundamentally the business model is flawed. For a two-sided marketplace to succeed in economic terms, both parties, merchants and consumers, must find it beneficial to use the platform. Showing little signs of improvement, reflected by double-digit growth in billing and positive free cash flow, investors turned bullish.

When my article got published, Groupon's share price was at $18; after the publication, the stock rose to almost $38, a 106% price appreciation in just 2 months. Since then the stock has corrected and currently stands at 16%, an 11% price drop since my write-up. The main takeaway was that even though a company could have weak fundamentals amid a broken business model, the price could easily move positively the minute the sentiment from investors changes.

YoY, Groupon returned 35.61%, outperforming the S&P 500 and Etsy (ETSY), which both returned 20.97% and 17.71%, respectively. And slightly underperformed eBay (EBAY) with roughly 5 percentage points. Looking at Q3 earnings results, Groupon has rolled off old convertible debt with a new one at a higher cost of capital, increasing their interest expenses. Amid the resolution of tax disputes with the Italian authorities, both events are estimated to reduce 2026 FCF (the tax dispute settlement alone is estimated to reduce FCF by $15 million).

In spite of these headwinds, revenue growth, operating margins, OCF, and FCF have flipped to being positive. The fundamentals on this name are starting to inflect, forcing me to rethink my bearish thesis; as a result, I am upgrading Groupon to a hold.

What Bulls are saying

To be less biased on names I was bearish on, I find it to be a healthy exercise to read bullish takes. One bullish thesis came from the fellow analyst Tunga Capital, stating the firm is working on improving merchant economics. One way the company is doing this is by improving its matching algorithm so that discount deals from merchants go to targeted consumers and by enforcing repeat purchases through WOW deals. Also, management stated efforts to improve the user experience of the mobile app.

I believe these efforts are truly a step in the right direction for the company; however, how and when these efforts will reflect positively in financial terms on a sustainable basis is yet to be known, but recently we are starting to see earning margins turning positive, which could imply improvement in merchant economics.

Financial Analysis

Q3 2025 Income Statement

When looking at the earnings results of the latest quarter, we see that Groupon grew North America revenue 10% YoY to $96.01 million, while the international market shrunk 3% to $26.82 million. In aggregate, this resulted in total revenue increasing 7% to $122.83 million, beating consensus estimates by $839,000.00. Cost of revenue declined 5% to $10.99 million, resulting in gross profits increasing 9% to $111.84 million and landing gross margins at 91%. Operating expenses increased 1% to $109.64 million. The growth was driven by marketing spend increasing by 14% and offset by a 6% decline from SG&A. Operating income for the quarter was positive, as witnessed by a 139% increase compared to the prior year quarter to the value of $2.19 million, landing EBIT margins at 1.78%. Other expenses increased drastically by more than 500% YoY to $98.72 million, and the main reason for such an increase was due to the accounting loss north of $100 million the firm realized from the extinguishment of debt; this resulted in the pretax loss of $6.53 million.

Adjusted EBITDA for the quarter was $18 million, and GAAP net loss was $118 million. Normalized EPS was -$0.21, and GAAP EPS was -$2.92, missing consensus estimates by $0.46 and $2.95, respectively. For the fourth quarter, the consensus is estimating revenues of $137.34 million (implying a 5% YoY growth) and positive GAAP EPS of $0.16, a substantial difference from an EPS of -$1.20 that was realized in Q4 2024. These estimates show analysts' expectation of improved fundamentals.

9-Month Income Statement

Looking at the 9-month duration period, we can see that revenue growth was almost flat (rising by just 1%) at $365.72 million, bolstered by a 3% growth from North America and offset by a 5% decline from international markets. Cost of revenue declined 8% to $33.15 million, resulting in gross profits increasing 2% to $332.56 million. Operating expenses declined 1% to $315.43 million; the decline was mainly driven by SG&A spending shrinking 9% to $198.15 million while marketing increased 15% to $117.28 million, leading to operating income increasing 179% to $17.13 million and landing operating margins at 4.68%, which is a substantially higher margin compared to Q3, illustrating that on a trailing basis, operating margins are expanding.

9-Month Cash Flow Statement

The results from the cash flow statement are the ones I was most looking forward to, as management stated improving free cash flow as one of its priority goals alongside adjusted EBITDA. On non-cash items we see a net $92.58 million cash add-back, highly influenced by a $99 million account loss that was recorded on the income statement, which resulted in the company recording a net loss of $89.16 million for the 9-month duration. Operating cash flow, however, increased 510% YoY to $7.89 million, offset by a cash outflow of $37.44 million from working capital needs. With CAPEX at $11.05 million, free cash flow was negative at $3.16 million.

Groupon fundamentals on a trailing basis show some improvements; revenue declines have stabilized to moderate growth, operating margins have flipped to being positive since 2024, and the same trend is true with operating and free cash flow.

One chart that really convinced me to rerate Groupon to a hold is the revenue growth chart on a quarterly basis. We can see that during the 2020-2023 period Groupon was experiencing massive revenue declines; during 2024 the decline rate decreased to single digits, and as of the last quarter, it flipped positive to 7%, a record growth since 2016. It's still not clear whether this positive growth rate will be sustained going into 2026; however, this shows significant improvements in the company's fundamentals.

Q3 Balance Sheet

Despite the massive cash balance of $238.5 million, the state of Groupon's balance sheet raises few concerns for me, especially now that total liabilities have just surpassed total assets by $59.4 million.

This sudden increase in liabilities was driven by the firm's action of rolling over old convertible debt with new one for a premium north of $75 million. This action was to extend debt maturities from 2026 and 2027 to 2030. Compared to old notes that totalled $170 million with a weighted coupon rate of 5.647%, these new notes are worth a principal value of $244 million (43% higher than the old principal value) and have a coupon rate of 4.875%. Totalling everything, this new debt has a higher cost of capital than the old one, which will increase Groupon's interest expenses and negatively affect the bottom line. The chart below shows that coming into 2025, Groupon maturities were nearing, and the company, doubting it might have enough liquidity to cover these maturities, was forced to issue new notes at a higher premium. Now the debt balance has increased to $343.6 million, with the current portion worth $33.6 million.

Valuation

Compared to peers, Groupon has always traded at a discount, which is not surprising to me given my previous stance on the firm's business model. During the mid-year 2025 stock rally, the company for the short run reached the EV/revenue multiple of Etsy, but the correction contracted the multiple back to near its 3-year rolling median. Historically we can see that the company still trades at a slight premium to its rolling median, with the median at 1.05 vs. its current multiple of 1.5. I think this is a fair valuation, and until the company improves the merchant economics, the discounted valuation to peers is still warranted.

Risk

Even though I'm upgrading my rating from a sell to a hold, we still don't have enough running data to confidently state that Groupon's fundamentals are at an inflection point. And given the $25 million settlement with the Italian tax authorities, I expect the bottom line and FCF to be pressured, which could lead to a further price decline.

Conclusion

Groupon is definitely an interesting company to write about. I believe the firm currently has a flawed business model, but recent fundamental improvements tell me the company might be working on improving merchant economics, which could be a substantial change if this goal gets achieved. What makes a two-sided marketplace thrive is its ability to offer a compelling value proposition to both the supply and the demand side. Historically, Groupon hasn't done a good job in serving the supply side, but recent improvements in revenue growth and operating margins suggest the company is doing something different to help out merchants. It's still too early to turn bullish; hence, I remain neutral until more evidence is presented.

data.title: Salesforce: Cheap 14x FCF Multiple And Agentforce Acceleration
data.description:
A few years ago, it would have been unthinkable to imagine that Salesforce (CRM), widely considered the original cloud software company, would trade at discounted multiples of earnings. Salesforce has long been known to be a growth-at-all-costs company, prioritizing product development and even splashy M&A deals to grow as large as possible.

Today, its operating mantra has flipped. While large-cap tech rivals like Microsoft (MSFT) and Meta (META) are hiring heavily and boosting capex to compete in the AI arms race, Salesforce has turned its focus to profitability. It's that more conservative operating nature that I think gives Salesforce a lot of credibility if the S&P 500 undergoes a bearish cycle this year, and why I'm keen to buy the stock after a ~25% slide over the past year.

I last wrote a "Buy" article on Salesforce in October, when the stock was trading slightly below $250 per share. Since then, Salesforce has delivered a beat-and-raise quarter in Q3, while also lifting its free cash flow expectations for the year. I continue to underline that Salesforce is now a true value stock, especially in the large-cap tech space where stretched multiples abound. Amid acceleration as well in the company's AI-driven Agentforce product, I'm reiterating my "Buy" rating on this company.

As a reminder for investors who are newer to this stock, here is my full long-term bull case on Salesforce:

  • Aggressive growth targets through FY30. Though from a stock market perspective Salesforce appears to have been left behind in the dust, the company has initiated very aggressive growth targets through FY30 (calendar 2029). The company aims to generate at least $60+ billion in revenue in that year, which would imply a ~10% growth CAGR from now until FY30. Given Salesforce usually has a track record for guiding conservatively, it's likely that the company will continue to nudge this goal upward. The fact that RPO (essentially backlog) is already growing at a low teens pace helps to provide support behind these targets.
  • Agentforce is a material growth driver. ARR across the company's "Agentforce" line of agentic AI-driven products is more than doubling y/y. Unlike many other software companies for whom AI is merely a buzzword without material growth to show for it, Salesforce is making a dent with its AI products - even if it isn't as loud, or as flashy with its spending as some of its other larger-cap peers.
  • Broad product portfolio to cross-sell with. In a time of budget tightness for many companies, many IT departments have implemented more vendor concentration: reducing the number of "point solution" tools that aim to address only a single issue. Salesforce has a huge product portfolio across sales, marketing, internal communications, APIs - essentially anything that's not ERP or backend infrastructure related, Salesforce has. This creates a powerful flywheel of products to cross-sell into customers and grow its wallet share.
  • Rule of 40. Salesforce is achieving ~10% revenue growth amid a mid-30s pro forma operating margin, positioning the stock easily within the Rule of 40 that many software companies aspire to.

There's an overwhelming narrative in the stock market that AI is out to disrupt the cloud software industry, which were themselves the disruptors of IT merely a few years back. To me, these fears are unfounded: while players like Claude's Anthropic are certainly making a dent in enterprise AI, there's no evidence yet that companies can rip out specialized applications like Salesforce's Sales Cloud in favor of a new enterprise AI app that can run those functions.

Take advantage of the ~20% slide over the past year as a buying opportunity.

Q3 download

Let's now go through Salesforce's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q3 earnings summary is shown below:

Salesforce's revenue grew 8.6% y/y to $10.26 billion, keeping virtually the same pace of growth as in Q2. The chart below, meanwhile, showcases the company's growth rates by product line:

Where we are impressed is the company's results in Sales (originally named Sales Cloud). Across the enterprise software sector, sales and marketing-related software products have seen compression in growth rates thanks to customers de-prioritizing growth (in other words, sales hiring is more challenging in today's environment). This has put pressure on seat counts. And yet, though Salesforce's core Sales product (which it's most famous for and is the industry standard CRM) is no longer exhibiting startup-like growth rates at its scale. We do note that Salesforce's marketing products are experiencing a bit more pressure, with growth decelerating to 1% year/y.

That said, this is more than offset by acceleration in Agentforce/platform revenue, which grew 19% y/y and has achieved four sequential quarters of acceleration. Meanwhile, the snapshot below showcases the company's direct ARR from gen AI and Agentforce products, which hit $540 million in Q3 and grew at a stunning 330% y/y pace. Again, AI isn't just a marketing ploy for Salesforce: it's actually generating superb revenue growth from its agentic AI products.

Another core indicator of strong growth is RPO, which is effectively backlog. Total RPO grew 12% y/y, while current RPO (which is the portion of backlog that will be recognized as revenue within the next 12 months) grew 11% y/y. Both of these are ahead of current revenue growth, and ahead of the company's implied 10% growth CAGR target through FY30 (specifically, getting from $41.50 billion in this year's FY26 guidance midpoint to $60 billion in FY30 requires 9.6% annual growth over the next four years).

The company notes that its strategy of having Salesforce sales reps "sell down" these products, i.e. explaining them to customers in a way they can understand, has helped fuel sales. The company has rebranded all of its core product lines as AI and agentic-capable. Per CEO Marc Benioff's remarks on the Q3 earnings call:

Agentforce and Data reached nearly $1.4 billion in ARR in the quarter, up 114% year-over-year, including Agentforce ARR of about $540 million, 330% year-over-year. And I think all of our account executives, I think we got about 15,000, I don't know what the exact number is out there, are all selling this down, where people really can understand it. We can demo it, we can show it [...]

And every Salesforce app now, not just sales, service, marketing, commerce, all of them, Tableau, Slack, our new ITSM, supply chain products, they've all been rebuilt, and Srini is here, he's going to talk about what we've done to bring Agentforce into every product we have. And they -- we transform Agentforce from being a product to a platform so that all of our apps can reason, learn, take action, collaborate with users, but it's really about humans and apps and the AI and the data all working together.

And unlike the scores of tech companies that are sacrificing profitability to invest in AI, Salesforce continues to expand with efficiency in mind. Pro forma operating margins in Q3 vaulted up 240bps yesterday/y to 35.5%.

On top of a 9% revenue growth rate, Salesforce easily clears the Rule of 40.

Valuation and key takeaways

And yet, despite its strong growth/profitability balance, the stock continues to trade at very discounted valuation multiples. At current share prices near $240, Salesforce trades at a market cap of $225.87 billion. Netting off the $11.32 billion of cash and $8.44 billion of debt on the company's latest balance sheet yields an enterprise value of $222.99 billion.

For the remainder of FY26 (which ends in January 2026), Salesforce has lifted its revenue guidance to $41.45-$41.55 billion, or 9-10% y/y growth. It's also now expecting 13-14% FCF growth, which implies $14.11 billion at the midpoint, or 34% FCF margins, up one point from 33% in FY25.

For next year FY27, Wall Street is expecting Salesforce to deliver $13.11 in pro forma EPS (+11% y/y) on $46.0 billion in revenue, also at 11% y/y growth. If we assume the same trend of 1 point of FCF margin expansion (to a 35% margin), FCF would be $16.10 billion (+14% y/y). This positions Salesforce's valuation multiples at:

  • 4.8x EV/FY27 revenue
  • 13.8x EV/FY27 FCF
  • 18.4x FY27 P/E

For a company with bona fide AI tailwinds, continued margin expansion, and a broad product portfolio, Salesforce remains a great "growth at a reasonable price" play; and in an expensive stock market this can even be considered a bargain. Don't hesitate to load up on this stock at current prices.

data.title: Steady Inflation Supports More Rate Cuts, But Markets Still See A Pause Ahead
data.description:
By James Picerno

US consumer inflation remained steady in December, providing the Federal Reserve with more room to lower interest rates. But market-based expectations suggest the central bank will remain cautious on further rate cuts for the near term after easing policy three times last year.

Although it’s not directly relevant, the news this week that the US Department of Justice launched a criminal investigation of Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell potentially adds another layer of uncertainty for assessing the outlook for monetary policy. But after reviewing a range of indicators, the outlook remains mixed for more rate cuts in the near term.

The key economic factors that will guide policy in 2026: inflation and the labor market. The latest numbers on these fronts suggest there’s still a case for more easing. Meanwhile, TMC Research’s Fed Funds Model indicates that current monetary policy remains moderately tight, which leaves the central bank with space for additional dovish adjustments.

Let’s start with inflation. The Bureau of Labor Statistics reports that consumer inflation held steady in December for the year-over-year trend. For both headline and core readings of the Consumer Price Index (CPI), pricing pressure stabilized from the previous month, rising 2.7% and 2.6% from the year-earlier level, respectively.

Although there are still data concerns for CPI due to lingering effects from the government shutdown, taking the numbers at face value suggests inflation remains sticky in the mid-2% range. That’s moderately above the Fed’s 2% target, but inflation isn’t reheating, which implies that rate cuts are still possible.

Combine the behaved CPI data with the slowdown in the labor market in the second half of last year, and a recipe for easing emerges, or so one can argue. Yet deciding why hiring has slowed will be crucial for deciding if more rate cuts are warranted.

At issue is whether the softer pace of hiring reflects business-cycle weakness vs. still-healthy growth that’s linked to a lower “breakeven” for job creation due to recent declines in US net immigration. Though the probability of finding a job hit a record low of 43.1% in December, according to the Federal Reserve Bank of New York’s latest Survey of Consumer Expectations, this lends slightly more credence to the former than the latter narrative.

Identifying which explanation is more influential (or whether both play a role) will likely take several more months before a consensus begins to emerge.

Meanwhile, TMC Research’s Fed funds model still shows monetary policy running moderately tight, implying that the target rate can be cut while still maintaining a slightly hawkish stance.

By our estimate, the current target rate is roughly 90 basis points above the optimal rate, which is our assessment of neutral for a set of financial and economic indicators. The current spread continues to reflect a middling degree of tightness compared to the range since September.

Market expectations continue to anticipate that the Fed will keep rates steady for the near term. Fed funds futures are estimating a high probability for no change at the next policy meeting scheduled for Jan. 28. Looking further out, the odds slide but still favor standing pat for the two subsequent meetings in March and April.

The bond market also suggests that the Fed may pause with rate cuts at the next meeting. In particular, the policy-sensitive 2-year yield is trading around 3.5%, which is also the low end of the current range for the Fed funds rate. The implication: the market is comfortable with the current target rate. That’s a significant change from recent history, when the 2-year yield traded well below the target rate, a spread that foreshadowed rate cuts.

Reasoned or not, the Fed looks set to keep rates steady for the near term. Although there’s a case for another cut or two, economic conditions are still muddled from the effects of the government shutdown and other factors. As new numbers arrive and the state of inflation and the labor market become clearer, the Fed will reassess the wisdom of cutting rates further or standing pat.

Original Source: The Milwaukee Company

data.title: Oakmark Equity And Income Fund Q4 2025 Portfolio Review
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from the Oakmark Equity And Income Fund Q4 2025 Commentary.

Top contributor

Alphabet (GOOG) was the top contributor during the quarter. The U.S.-headquartered technology conglomerate’s stock price jumped after it delivered impressive third-quarter earnings. Search revenue growth beat consensus expectations as Google continues to see strong user engagement trends. Results were also ahead of expectations in the Cloud segment, and the outlook for this business remains robust given accelerating demand for AI compute. We continue to believe Alphabet is undervalued on a sum-of-the-parts basis and see potential for the company’s AI leadership to drive further upside across the portfolio.

Top detractor

Fiserv (FISV) was the top detractor during the quarter. The U.S.-headquartered bank software and payment processor’s stock price declined sharply after reporting disappointing third-quarter earnings. Following the completion of a recent strategic review, Fiserv’s new CEO concluded that prior leadership’s medium-term revenue growth and margin targets were unachievable. While we are disappointed by these revelations, we continue to believe that Fiserv is a formidable competitor with attractive assets operating in growing end markets. We are also encouraged by recent upgrades to the executive management team and the board of directors, as well as recent insider stock purchases. The new management team expects Fiserv to deliver mid-single-digit organic revenue growth and double-digit earnings per share (EPS) growth on a normalized basis. Accordingly, we believe the risk/reward is attractive with the shares trading at a single-digit multiple of current EPS.

New purchases

Amrize (AMRZ) is a leading producer of building materials and construction solutions, established following Holcim's spin-off of its North American operations in June. As a standalone company, Amrize holds strong market positions—it is the largest cement producer, the second-largest commercial roofing products manufacturer and a top-five aggregates producer in the U.S. and Canada. The company benefits from pricing power due to its scale, advantaged regional footprint and tight supply dynamics. We also see long-term potential in the Building Envelope segment and believe there is a path to narrowing the margin gap to roofing peers. In our view, Amrize is a fundamentally strong business that is flying under the radar of most U.S. investors. This created the opportunity to invest alongside a proven management team focused on unlocking shareholder value.

Gartner (IT) is a global leader in research services, with a long history of delivering valuable insights and data to business and technology leaders. In our view, the company has the best brand in IT research, supported by its scale and a compelling customer value proposition. These advantages have driven a long history of strong organic growth and robust free-cash-flow conversion. The stock price has declined meaningfully from recent highs due to investor concerns surrounding AI-related disruption. We believe these concerns are overstated. In our view, Gartner is well-positioned to reaccelerate organic growth due to continued high customer engagement and the large opportunity to sell to new and existing customers. We took advantage of the opportunity to buy shares in this well-managed company at a bargain price.

Mondelēz International (MDLZ) is a global snacking powerhouse with leading market share positions in crackers, cookies and chocolate. The brand portfolio houses iconic names like Cadbury, Milka, Toblerone, Oreo and Ritz. Mondelēz possesses a unique global footprint that over-indexes to snacking occasions. Snacking is an advantaged category that benefits from robust pricing power, low private label competition and rising per capita consumption. We believe these attributes will help Mondelēz sustain industry-leading growth. A rapid rise in commodity costs has temporarily depressed margins, masking the company’s true earnings power. We believe Mondelēz’s strong pricing power and commodity relief will help improve margins. The short-term fears surrounding commodity inflation allowed us to purchase shares at a discounted valuation relative to history, peers and the broader market.

data.title: Parker-Hannifin: One Of The Best Around... And Priced Like It
data.description:
What do you do with a company that's among the best in its class when it comes to running its business and creating long-term shareholder value, but is priced accordingly?

That's a relevant question when it comes to Parker-Hannifin (PH) ("Parker"), as I still consider this one of the best industrial companies out there. In addition to strong margins and returns (ROIC, et al), management has a demonstrated track record of "sticking to its knitting" and pursuing core competencies and value-creation strategies rather than chasing whatever happens to be hot today. The "but" is that that valuation reflects these positives.

Parker shares are up more than 150% since my last update, handily beating the broader industrial sector and its peer group, with only a handful of names really rivaling those returns, and most of those (like ABB (ABBNY) and General Electric (GE)) having a meaningful restructuring/turnaround story or thematic angle (Caterpillar (CAT), Moog (MOG-A)) involved.

At this point I can't say that the valuation on Parker-Hannifin is anything like discounted, but I openly acknowledge that may well not matter for some time to come.

Signs Of Life In Short-Cycle, With Strong Aero Tailwinds

Expectations may be the biggest challenge for Parker in the coming quarter and year, particularly in light of a forward P/E of almost 31x on what is expected to be mid-single-digit revenue growth.

On the positive side of the ledger, I expect another strong year of aerospace demand. Major OEMs like Airbus (EADSY) and Boeing (BA) appear to have ironed out a lot of their production bottlenecks and there are still robust orderbooks of narrowbody and widebody aircraft to fulfill. Beyond this are ongoing growth opportunities in defense, as nothing in the geopolitical landscape today suggests that countries are going to be spending meaningfully less on armaments.

Looking specifically at the next quarter, I expect double-digit growth for the Aerospace segment, but I'd note that defense aftermarket has been something of a headwind, leading to lower revenue growth relative to peers like Eaton (ETN), GE, Honeywell (HON), and Moog with incrementally different business mixes.

The calendar third quarter was a bit surprising for the industrial sector in general, with unexpected signs of life in short-cycle industrial after a prolonged period of destocking and weak demand. Parker reported 2% growth in the North America segment and 1% growth in the International segment, with order growth of 3% and 6%, respectively.

Although I am not expecting a V-shaped recovery, I do think another 2%+ result in North America and 1%+ in International is possible, and I think there is some upside potential from restocking, as industrial distributor MSC Industrial (MSM) did manage 4% growth in the quarter and Fastenal (FAST) and been seeing some improvements in demand trends as well.

Margin performance is likely to be less dramatic, if only because Parker has a pretty good history of managing expectations and delivering accordingly. The Street is looking for 25.7% segment margins for the quarter and I think they could do a little better, but estimates have gone higher after a surprisingly strong fiscal first quarter where segment income beat by 8%, with each line-item beating.

Most Markets Are Picking Up

Looking across the breadth of Parker's addressed markets, there really aren't too many areas that concern me overly much, and the weak areas I do see are already well-understood by the market as weak areas.

As I mentioned above, aerospace should be in for another strong year, and I think there's a solid chance that Parker could once again deliver double-digit growth here as the major OEMs look to accelerate their build rates and as defense projects move forward.

Within the broad industrial space, I think the first half will see a tentative recovery with a lot of variability among specific end-markets. I expect activity to pick up as the year goes on, though, and I think low-single-digit growth is a reasonable expectation today.

Demand in areas like fluid control still looks pretty healthy (considering guidance/commentary from ITT (ITT), et al), and Parker's leverage to automation should offer some upside to underlying industrial production, as companies like ABB and Rockwell (ROK) have called out improving automation markets. I also expect improving demand in food/bev, pharma, and data center, but these are relatively small businesses for Parker today (though food/bev and pharma are getting bigger with the Filtration Group acquisition).

HVAC and refrigeration should, by comparison, be an area of relative strength. Management's expectation for mid-single-digit growth seems reasonable, even against a backdrop of weak housing activity and still-weak commercial construction starts, as renovation demand is looking good.

Off-highway won't likely be all that strong, as I see pressures in mobile mining and ag equipment, but I do expect construction equipment to start picking up, and this is not an insignificant business at around 6% of revenue. Transport, though, is likely to be weak, with truck demand still not healthy and auto production rates likely to be soft in North America and Europe.

Lastly, energy should be okay, with much stronger demand in generating turbines.

A Sound Strategy Remains In Place

The good, and bad, of Parker today is that it's still quite sensitive to overall economic conditions. The Industrial segments have historically correlated strongly (over 85%) with underlying U.S. and global industrial production, and it's worth remembering that core organic growth has actually been quite modest this century (around 200bp/year on average). Expanding into aerospace has pulled that up in recent years, and I expect other acquisitions like Filtration Group to help as well, but I think it's worth remembering the past as it does illustrate how there are still some exposures to slower-growing legacy markets here.

Strong operating margin performance offsets some of those historical growth issues, as the company has been quite good at implementing strategic margin-boosting initiatives like the "Win Strategy". What's more, I like management's consistent focus on the real core of the business - "small ticket" components that are nevertheless mission critical, and where customers will typically pay up for reliability and not complain too much about pricing.

I also note that management has been consistent in its overall strategic focus. Parker has a good sense of what it does well, and pretty much everything it does is built around hydraulics, pneumatics, electromechanical, filtration, fluid/gas handling, process control, climate control, or engineered materials.

That's important given that a lot of other supposed "compounder" companies have pivoted multiple times toward whatever was popular (software-based models, healthcare, analytics/predictive maintenance tools, life sciences, clean energy) and without a real sense of what their core competencies were. It's also important because Parker has always been an acquisitive company and this willingness to stick to its core has helped drive success from M&A where many peers/comps have failed, and it lends confidence in future M&A activity.

The Outlook

I'm looking for about 6% revenue growth this year, and that puts me a bit above the Street right now. With the inclusion of Filtration Group I expect reported revenue growth to accelerate to nearly 8% over the next five years before settling around 6% to 7% long term. Unlike most companies, I bake in an expected M&A contribution to longer-term revenue forecast. I don't think Parker will do another major deal in the next 18 months, but I think future large deals are all but certain.

On the margin side, I believe Parker will get close to mid-30%'s EBITDA over the next five years, and that in turn should drive high-teens free cash flow margins and high single-digit free cash flow growth. That, in turn, should continue to support ongoing increases in the dividend, as Parker has never been especially inclined to do meaningful buybacks (the share count is down about 5% over the past decade).

Valuation is the problem. Discounted cash flow modeling only gets me to a prospective mid-single-digit annualized return. Likewise, even if I give the company credit above and beyond its margins and returns, including premiums to the EBITDA multiple for its growth and overall long-term quality, a 21.75x forward multiple still only gets me to about today's price and that's a lot to pay for mid-teens EBITDA growth. Going a year out (so I can add in a full year of Filtration Group) and discounting back at 8% gets me to a little over $1,000, but that's a lot of pushing on valuation just to find about 10% of undervaluation.

The Bottom Line

I have no doubt that there are readers who will say they don't care about the valuation and that Parker's quality growth will see them through to further gains. Fair enough. But I'd note that I heard the same arguments not that long ago about Danaher (DHR), Fortive (FTV), Illinois Tool Works (ITW), and Roper (ROP), and not one of them has outperformed the broader industrial sector over the past five years (the best return in the bunch is 25% for ITW versus more than 80% for the sector).

I wouldn't short Parker without a good reason, and given the strength in the aerospace market and the revivals I expect in shorter-cycle industrials, the business should have tailwinds in the near term and I don't see those good reasons. By the same token, though, overpaying is a great way to compromise returns and I'd rather find other ideas that aren't so robustly valued today.

data.title: RSPD: Consumer Discretionary Dashboard For January
data.description:
This article offers a top-down analysis of the consumer discretionary sector, based on industry metrics focusing on value, quality, and momentum. It may also help analyze sector ETFs such as the Consumer Discretionary Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLY) and the Invesco S&P 500 Equal Weight Consumer Discretionary ETF (RSPD), whose holdings are used to calculate these metrics.

Shortcut

The next two paragraphs in italics describe the dashboard methodology. They are necessary for new readers to understand the metrics. If you are used to this series or if you are short of time, you can skip them and go to the charts.

Base Metrics

I calculate the median value of five fundamental ratios for each subsector: Earnings Yield ("EY"), Sales Yield ("SY"), Free Cash Flow Yield ("FY"), Return on Equity ("ROE"), Gross Margin ("GM"). The reference universe includes large companies in the U.S. stock market. The five base metrics are calculated on trailing 12 months. For all of them, higher is better. EY, SY and FY are medians of the inverse of Price/Earnings, Price/Sales and Price/Free Cash Flow. They are better for statistical studies than price-to-something ratios, which are unusable or non available when the "something" is close to zero or negative (for example, companies with negative earnings). I also look at two momentum metrics for each group: the median monthly return (RetM) and the median annual return (RetY).

I prefer medians to averages because a median splits a set in a good half and a bad half. A capital-weighted average is skewed by extreme values and the largest companies. My metrics are designed for stock-picking rather than index investing.

Value and Quality Scores

I calculate historical baselines for all metrics. They are noted respectively EYh, SYh, FYh, ROEh, GMh, and they are calculated as the averages on a look-back period of 11 years. For example, the value of EYh for retailing in the table below is the 11-year average of the median earnings yield in retail companies.

The Value Score ("VS") is defined as the average difference in % between the three valuation ratios (EY, SY, FY) and their baselines (EYh, SYh, FYh). In the same way, the Quality Score ("QS") is the average difference between the two quality ratios (ROE, GM) and their baselines (ROEh, GMh).

The scores are in percentage points. VS may be interpreted as the percentage of undervaluation or overvaluation relative to the baseline (positive is good, negative is bad). This interpretation must be taken with caution: the baseline is an arbitrary reference, not a supposed fair value. The formula assumes that the three valuation metrics are of equal importance. A floor of -100 is set for VS and QS when the calculation goes below this value. It may happen when metrics in a subsector are very bad.

data.title: Oakmark Equity And Income Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Fourth Quarter, 2025

  • The Fund (Investor Class) outperformed the benchmark, the 60% S&P 500 / 40% Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index, for the quarter and since inception.
  • The equity portfolio returned 5.081 for the quarter versus 2.66% for the S&P 500 Index. The fixed income portfolio returned 1.35% versus 1.10% for the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index. The Fund’s allocation was 60.0% in equities, 38.5% in fixed income, and 1.5% cash.
  • At the sector level for the equity portfolio, the largest contributors to performance were communication services and health care, while industrials was the only detractor.
  • The Fund had a strong quarter as the breadth of the market rally improved, with many value and smaller capitalization stocks performing well. We continue to find attractive opportunities to invest in undervalued companies across a variety of industries, including areas that have been left behind in the past year’s momentum rally.

Fourth-quarter highlights

Top stock contributors

  • Alphabet Cl A (GOOG)
  • Warner Bros Discovery (WBD)
  • Glencore (GLCNF)

Top stock detractors

  • Fiserv (FISV)
  • Equifax (EFX)
  • Charter Communications Cl A (CHTR)

New stock purchases

  • Amrize (AMRZ)
  • Gartner (IT)
  • Mondelēz Cl A (MDLZ)

Final stock sales

  • BlackRock (BLK)
  • Charter Communications Cl A

Top Contributor | Detractor

Top contributor

Alphabet was the top contributor during the quarter. The U.S.-headquartered technology conglomerate’s stock price jumped after it delivered impressive third-quarter earnings. Search revenue growth beat consensus expectations as Google continues to see strong user engagement trends. Results were also ahead of expectations in the Cloud segment, and the outlook for this business remains robust given accelerating demand for AI compute. We continue to believe Alphabet is undervalued on a sum-of-the-parts basis and see potential for the company’s AI leadership to drive further upside across the portfolio.

Top detractor

Fiserv was the top detractor during the quarter. The U.S.-headquartered bank software and payment processor’s stock price declined sharply after reporting disappointing third-quarter earnings. Following the completion of a recent strategic review, Fiserv’s new CEO concluded that prior leadership’s medium-term revenue growth and margin targets were unachievable. While we are disappointed by these revelations, we continue to believe that Fiserv is a formidable competitor with attractive assets operating in growing end markets. We are also encouraged by recent upgrades to the executive management team and the board of directors, as well as recent insider stock purchases. The new management team expects Fiserv to deliver mid-single-digit organic revenue growth and double-digit earnings per share (EPS) growth on a normalized basis. Accordingly, we believe the risk/reward is attractive with the shares trading at a single-digit multiple of current EPS.

Portfolio Activity

New purchases

Amrize is a leading producer of building materials and construction solutions, established following Holcim's spin-off of its North American operations in June. As a standalone company, Amrize holds strong market positions—it is the largest cement producer, the second-largest commercial roofing products manufacturer and a top-five aggregates producer in the U.S. and Canada. The company benefits from pricing power due to its scale, advantaged regional footprint and tight supply dynamics. We also see long-term potential in the Building Envelope segment and believe there is a path to narrowing the margin gap to roofing peers. In our view, Amrize is a fundamentally strong business that is flying under the radar of most U.S. investors. This created the opportunity to invest alongside a proven management team focused on unlocking shareholder value.

Gartner is a global leader in research services, with a long history of delivering valuable insights and data to business and technology leaders. In our view, the company has the best brand in IT research, supported by its scale and a compelling customer value proposition. These advantages have driven a long history of strong organic growth and robust free-cash-flow conversion. The stock price has declined meaningfully from recent highs due to investor concerns surrounding AI-related disruption. We believe these concerns are overstated. In our view, Gartner is well-positioned to reaccelerate organic growth due to continued high customer engagement and the large opportunity to sell to new and existing customers. We took advantage of the opportunity to buy shares in this well-managed company at a bargain price.

Mondelēz International is a global snacking powerhouse with leading market share positions in crackers, cookies and chocolate. The brand portfolio houses iconic names like Cadbury, Milka, Toblerone, Oreo and Ritz. Mondelēz possesses a unique global footprint that over-indexes to snacking occasions. Snacking is an advantaged category that benefits from robust pricing power, low private label competition and rising per capita consumption. We believe these attributes will help Mondelēz sustain industry-leading growth. A rapid rise in commodity costs has temporarily depressed margins, masking the company’s true earnings power. We believe Mondelēz’s strong pricing power and commodity relief will help improve margins. The short-term fears surrounding commodity inflation allowed us to purchase shares at a discounted valuation relative to history, peers and the broader market.

The 60% S&P 500 / 40% Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index is comprised of 60% weight given to the S&P 500 Index and 40% weight given to the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index and is rebalanced monthly. It is a widely recognized index of broad, U.S. equity market performance. The Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index is a broad-based benchmark that measures the investment grade, U.S. dollar-denominated, fixed-rate taxable bond market. The index includes Treasuries, government-related and corporate securities, mortgage-backed securities (agency fixed-rate and hybrid ARM pass-throughs), asset-backed securities and commercial mortgage-backed securities (agency and non-agency). These indexes are unmanaged and investors cannot invest directly in them.

Investing involves risk; principal loss is possible. There is no guarantee the Fund's investment objective will be achieved. Value stocks may fall out of favor with investors and underperform growth stocks during given periods.

Medium- and lower-quality debt securities that have higher yield potential but present greater investment and credit risk than higher-quality securities which may result in greater share price volatility. An economic downturn could severely disrupt the market in medium or lower grade debt securities and adversely affect the value of outstanding bonds and the ability of the issuers to repay principal and interest. The Fund’s portfolio tends to be invested in a relatively small number of stocks. As a result, the appreciation or depreciation of any one security held by the Fund will have a greater impact on the Fund’s net asset value than it would if the Fund invested in a larger number of securities. Although that strategy has the potential to generate attractive returns over time, it also increases the Fund’s volatility.

Medium-sized companies’ stocks tend to be more volatile than those of large companies and have underperformed the stocks of small and large companies during some periods. These and other risk considerations are described in detail in the Fund’s prospectus

data.title: Inflation Is Sticky, Yet The Market Doesn't Care - Or Does It?
data.description:
December CPI data is out, and the results are slightly better than what the market expected:

  • Core CPI YoY 2.6% vs. 2.7% expected

  • Core CPI MoM 0.2% vs. 0.3% expected

With inflation still well above the Fed’s theoretical 2% target, it would be fair to think we are seeing a sticky metric. That is even more evident when putting the latest data in a 1-year context:

For the entirety of 2025, US inflation has been well above 2% and much closer to 3% (which, I argued in the past, may anyway turn out to be the Fed’s new target). The last time CPI was below the 2% target was February 2021, almost five years ago. In other words, we have now gone almost half a decade with inflation above target.

Despite this, equity markets have been rallying since late 2023 (even if through a few deep but quick corrections, like that of April 2025). Have markets been wrong in predicting lower inflation (and lower interest rates) for more than two years? Or have they simply stopped caring about inflation?

Today, I argue markets do care about inflation. And they have been right about seeing it cool off. However, they have been too early in discounting a return to a long-term Zero-Interest-Rate policy.

US domestic disinflationary forces will manifest in 2026

There are a number of reasons to be positive about inflation coming down in the next few months.

First of all, there is the impact of US trade tariffs on inflation. With most tariffs in full effect since August, I deem it unlikely we will see any spike in consumer prices in the upcoming months due to tariffs. While personally I remain of the opinion tariffs serve no purpose and provide no upside to Americans, I recognize their inflationary effect has most likely run its course.

And that’s without considering the option that tariffs may even get rolled back as a result of the Supreme Court declaring them “illegal” in their current form. If that were to happen, it would represent not a neutral but a disinflationary matter.

Then, we have the situation with oil, Venezuela, and Russia. Oil prices have a direct connection with consumer inflation, and they are close to their lowest in five years. Lower prices are at least partially a result of the current administration’s recent actions, as well as declining global demand (on the back of secular disinflationary forces I will discuss shortly).

Venezuela’s US-led regime change, while still ongoing, may help unlock oil supply that was previously severely limited due to US sanctions. Additionally, America seizing the “shadow fleet” of Russia may put pressure on Russia’s regime to bring the war in Ukraine to an end. Were that to happen, and were sanctions on Russia to be scaled back as a result, we would see even more natural gas and oil reaching the market.

Importantly, there is also the issue of the labor market. The Fed’s mandate is not exclusively about inflation but equally about full employment. With unemployment data creeping up closer to 5%, the Fed may have further room to cut rates, regardless of whether inflation stays closer to 3% than their target. In fact, a weak job market has been one of the reasons why equity markets kept rallying in late 2025, despite somewhat sticky inflation.

Purely in terms of inflation, a weak labor market may also act as a disinflationary force per se, as people out of a job (or earning less in real terms) tend to consume less. While US wage growth has so far kept up with inflation, this may also change with the advent of the next AI wave. AI has already been blamed for replacing jobs, even if I think evidence at the macro level remains thin so far. 2026 will be the year when we finally understand the full impact of AI on employment and productivity.

Finally, there are some of Trump’s recent policies, including the idea of blocking institutional investors from buying homes. In my view, it is clear some of the administration’s recent moves are geared towards fighting inflation, potentially ahead of Mid-Terms. It remains to be seen to what extent these initiatives may help, but I think we are now seeing an administration that is actively trying to fight inflation.

Global demographic forces remain a secular tailwind for America

I find it is sometimes easy to get lost in analyzing US-specific data without considering the broader global picture. The US only weighs about 25% of the world’s GDP. Even in the (arguably) post-globalization world we live in, America is still very much influenced by global trends.

I see the US as a unicum, in that it is a developed, large, high-growth, highly productive country with demographic issues that are not nearly as bad as those in most other developed countries. The rest of the world looks very, very different.

Demographic declines are a reality everywhere except in Africa. The US is the only developed, large country that is set to remain where it is in terms of number of people in 2100 (see infochart below).

A collapsing demographic is a strong disinflationary (and potentially even deflationary) force.

Readers can look at the examples of Japan and China, with the first having already undergone a major demographic collapse and China having recently been overtaken by India (assuming official Chinese numbers are even real in the first place). Japan has seen very low inflation for the past three decades, while China is currently battling deflation. In my view, Europe is set to follow suit in the next decade, representing yet another global disinflationary force. Even India, the world’s most populous country today, is set to see its population shrinking in the upcoming decades.

America will benefit from these trends in terms of inflation. Indirectly, via a decrease in demand for commodities and energy. As well as directly, with other nations exporting to its vibrant domestic market for lack of a sufficiently large domestic market.

Risks: The AI question and a stagflationary scenario

The main wild card for 2026, in my view, remains AI. AI may help America get out of sticky inflation, or it may worsen the situation. The below table, taken from my previous work, summarizes the possible effects of AI scenarios on the job market, inflation, and equities.

A stagflationary scenario would be the worst possible outcome for the US economy in 2026. This may manifest if, for example, AI productivity gains “overheat” the economy and cause inflation while not leading to mass unemployment. AI demand in itself, barring any other effect, may turn inflationary by virtue of increased energy demand (a core component of inflation).

With such uncertainty, I invite readers to review my 2026 market outlook article. There, I discuss in depth the economic and market implications of AI developing in 2026 and reaching a level close to AGI (Artificial General Intelligence).

Conclusion: markets are right, inflation is cooling off.

Barring an AI wild card, I think we are going to see inflation subdued in 2026, courtesy of:

  • Tariffs-induced inflation has run its course

  • Cheaper oil prices

  • A weaker labor market

  • Secular global disinflationary forces on the back of a global demographic crisis

Truflation, a proprietary inflation metric that is supposed to be less lagging than CPI, is already reporting data below 2%.

While this indicator has been way more volatile than CPI, it has done a decent job in recent years at “anticipating” future readings. For example, Truflation was already below 2% for a few weeks in late Q1 2025, before Trump’s “Liberation Day”.

In this context, I remain bullish on the S&P 500 and, more broadly, on US equities. I think markets have been correctly discounting a return to ZIRP, but they have so far been wrong about timing. 2026 will prove them right.

data.title: ClearBridge Global Infrastructure Income Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Key Takeaways

  • Markets: Listed infrastructure trailed global equities in the fourth quarter, both delivering a more subdued end to a strong year of performance.
  • Contributors: On a regional basis, Western Europe was the top portfolio contributor for quarter, with U.K. electric utility SSE and Spanish electric utility Iberdrola the lead performers.
  • Detractors: Higher production and storage levels and weaker weather forecasts for natural gas kept prices lower most of the quarter, weighing on shares of natural gas utilities and pipeline stocks. Elevated interest rates and lower growth drove largely negative performance from communication tower stocks.
  • Outlook: Inflection in electricity demand and solid earnings growth helped listed infrastructure performance in 2025, and this looks set to continue in 2026, with the added benefit of lower nominal bond yields.

Performance Review

  • On a regional basis, Western Europe was the top portfolio contributor for quarter, with U.K. electric utility SSE and Spanish electric utility Iberdrola the lead performers.
  • SSE (SSEZY) is a diversified energy utility headquartered in Scotland. It is vertically integrated, operating over the entire supply chain in the U.K., with generation (including hydro, wind, combined cycle gas turbine), electricity networks, and retail businesses (primarily B2B). It is the U.K.’s largest renewable energy generator. SSE’s share price rose as funding risks diminished and concerns around the U.K. macroeconomic outlook eased.
  • Iberdrola (IBDSF) is a multinational integrated electric utility company headquartered in Spain and engaged in energy networks, renewables and wholesale and retail operations. The company has expanded internationally with operations in the U.K. (via Scottish Power), the U.S. (via Avangrid), Brazil (via Neoenergia (NRGIY)) and Mexico. Iberdrola delivered a solid capital markets day, with management raising guidance again at its third-quarter results.
  • U.S. electric utility OGE Energy (OGE) and Spanish electric utility Redeia (RDEIF) were the largest detractors.
  • OGE Energy operates the largest utility business in Oklahoma. Delays in the announcement of a data center deal weighed on shares during the quarter.
  • Redeia is engaged in the supply and transmission of electricity and is the sole high-voltage transmission agent and system operator in Spain. Negative sentiment surrounding a suboptimal outcome from the Spanish regulatory review for 2026–2031 weighed on Redeia’s share price.

Outlook

  • Inflection in electricity demand and solid earnings growth helped listed infrastructure performance in 2025, and this looks set to continue in 2026, with the added benefit of lower nominal bond yields.
  • Electric utilities, which make up the bulk of our portfolio, continue to bene1t from several tailwinds: the energy transition, as poles and wires are built out to connect renewables to the grid and EV charging stations (all regulated expenditure for utilities and earning regulated returns); the mitigation of and adaptation to climate change impacts on electricity networks, which is driving resiliency spend; and growing electricity demand, particularly from AI data centers, but also from growing industrial demand. These tailwinds support a strong earnings backdrop and continued conviction in a balanced exposure to utilities.

data.title: Plains All Americans: The High-Yield Crude Option
data.description:
Introduction

Plains All American (NASDAQ: PAA/PGP) has gone through a consequent strategic move, narrowing its pure-play crude focus since I last reviewed the company back in early August. Though it has appreciated on par with the S&P00 since, I want to see if the company has made the required improvements to become a buy in my books. Right now, Plains is one of the largest midstream companies in the country that has a great presence in the Permian and an 18,000+ mile pipeline network.

Current Dynamics

Following its Canadian NGL divestment earlier in 2025, PAA has gone further into being a pure-play crude midstream as it markets itself. PAA bought the EPIC crude pipeline to further dominate the Permian. PAA executed a two-step takeover for a total of $2.9B, including debt, and will integrate it into its network as Cactus III. The advantage, aside from increased volume throughput, would be that the company can now switch barrels between lines to optimize flow, reducing the use of expensive drag-reducing agents and offering more flexible shipping options to different export docks in Corpus Christi.

Current capacity for the line is set at roughly 600K bpd but could be expanded to 900K simply by adding more pumping stations, so PAA could incur low expansion costs if there is still demand for it. Although the company will pay the sellers up to $350MM in addition to earnout payments if it does so. The deal is set to trade at around 10x EBITDA multiples that can drop to 8x thanks to synergies and the expected increase in throughput.

There is some good and some bad with the deal in my opinion, as the company now controls the most efficient route from the Permian to the export hubs of Corpus Christi, leaving the ability to bundle their services for big producers. It will be immediately accretive to DCF and PAA is expecting mid-teens unlevered return on the investment. On the flip side, the deal spiked leverage for the company as it closed before the Canadian NGL sale finished, meaning that for the time being, the company’s leverage ratio is sitting above its target, though that is only transitory.

One move that the company has made to remediate this would be the one-time special distribution of around 35 cents per unit to reassure unitholders, as the Canadian NGL business sale is a taxable event. More so, the company is expecting to pay roughly $360MM in entity-level taxes in Canada, which is actually good news for US unitholders, as it will most likely generate foreign tax credits that can be passed through to unitholders and their K-1s to offset the US tax hit.

Now, as the company is positioning itself as a crude pure-play, it has become more sensitive to volume shifts in the Permian and global export demand than ever before. The Permian is still producing over 6MM bpd, but the era of double-digit annual growth is most likely over, as analysts are expecting US production to hover around 13.5MM through 2026, and that’s why the company has pivoted towards a market share approach rather than market growth.

Following the US operation in Venezuela and the current administration signalling plans to move directly 30 to 50 million barrels of heavy crude in storage, it redirects future production towards US Gulf Coast refineries. Outside of the difficulties to ramp up production in the country, if refineries can get cheap oil that is more tailored towards their complexity levels than demand for light Permian barrels might slow down, creating bottlenecks through PAA’s system.

Valuation

Plains compares fairly well with its peers with its EV/EBITDA of 9.8, which is the second lowest in the group, but where the company really shines through is its current FCF generation that allows the company to have one of the best FCF yields in the industry at 17.7%, and this trend has been going on since 2020. Right now, the company has shifted from the past growth-at-all-costs mode to a harvest mode that allows it to directly find opportunities that make FCF skyrocket. Though the company might suffer from a pure-play valuation discount that is tied to the current state of the oil market. Looking at distribution, PAA boasts one of the highest yields at 8.09%, though it slightly decreases from the 9% we’ve seen earlier in 2025. The distribution has extensively grown over the past 3 years at a rate of 22.22% CAGR, and management has announced a massive 10% distribution hike for the year that excludes the special 35 cents, which will make it a gigantic year for income-focused unitholders.

The rebranded Cactus III is the main growth engine heading into 2026, which could push earnings growth at roughly 15% per year through 2027, which is currently forecasted, though revenue growth is expected to be much flatter. Now I believe that this can change when PAA closes the $3.75B Canadian sale in the first quarter, which will significantly drop the company’s leverage ratio. The company can either use the proceeds to further expand its footprint in the Permian, boosting margins, or use that lower leverage to boost the company’s credit rating upgrades, lowering their borrowing costs, and in the end have better economics for deals further down the line.

PAA is undergoing a quality over quantity shift, and that’s why, in part, margins are fairly low, as highlighted by the 4.9% EBITDA margin. The key will be the Canadian NGL business sale, which used to be way more "commodity exposed" and pushed profits to swing widely. This is now replaced by the EPIC crude pipeline "take-or-pay" cash flow. It is also very important for investors to grasp why margins are so low compared to peers, something I did not grasp in my earlier analysis. See PAA, compared to others, acts as a massive middleman in the crude market, buying physical oil at the wellhead and selling it to refineries. If it buys $100 worth of oil and sells it for $102, it will only generate a 2% margin. While other companies in the space generally don’t buy the oil and just charge a fee to move it, thus drastically improving their margins. That’s why there is such a difference in the actual FCF PAA is generating.

Risks

The risk profile for PAA has significantly narrowed to the crude industry, mainly in the Permian. The shift towards a potentially heavier crude mix for refineries might be the biggest risk for PAA, especially if it has to lower shipping rates to keep its pipelines full. More so if Permian producers stop drilling due to the inherently lower price, then PAA could see far lower volumes in the future, thus lower sales.

It also has no Plan B if the world moves away from crude faster than expected, as the company is simply now a proxy of future volume, mainly in the Permian. I believe that the company’s position is solid, but investors fearing this should inherently choose other well-diversified midstreams.

Conclusion

I believe that right now, PAA can be a strong choice for investors looking to have a massive yield based on huge FCF generation, only tied to one industry. Mainly, if you’re bullish on the continuous volumes produced in the Permian, PAA should be your play, and it will reward you handsomely starting with its distribution. I upgrade my rating to a Buy.
